title: Twitter @oskar_hallstrom
summary: As seen from the figure, the early stages of training was actually biased towards data samples with lower id:s, and thus biased towards specific subsets of the data. After searching on the web, it seems like others also identified the same issue, such as @allen_ai w/ OLMo. (9/10)

title: Twitter @oskar_hallstrom
summary: Then, the id:s of a large enough subsequence of torch.randperm(n) should be evenly distributed over the bins. For n=1M, the first 10k elements of the shuffled array indeed contain a close to equal amount of id:s from each bin. Same went for the last 10k elements. Dang. (7/10) https://t.co/wgMlPrwwS1

title: Twitter @oskar_hallstrom
summary: Background: In native PyTorch data samplers, torch.randperm is used to select the order of which the samples of a dataset should be retrieved when shuffling is used. torch.randperm(n) generates a random permutation of ints from 0 to n-1. (2/10)
